two boys now?

Congratulations.
Thank you.
Thank you, thank you.

I'm really good.
I've been just chugging along and, you know, lots of things.
I'm just...
Thank
I've seen you travel everywhere on Instagram.
Oh, yeah.
So basically when the pandemic happened, Katie went on a train leave.
And because of what I do, I knew that we weren't going to go back to the office.
And so I kind of gave Audrey and Katie an ultimatum saying, if you don't let me leave San Francisco and be anywhere I want, I'm going to quit.
And they're like, go, go be free.
But yeah.
So I went to like, I went to Florida for a while to be closer to my family.
Both of my parents were in Miami during the pandemic.
And I was like, let me just go there and be with them for a while.
And then I went to San Diego.
And do you, did you ever meet or remember Emma Mescal?
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
So her and her husband.
She's right?
Yeah.

And then, um, and then I started to like.
And I traveling and I was like, I'll do this for a year and three kind of years later.
But it was interesting because I ended up going to Anthropic a year ago and they were okay with me not being fully based in San Francisco.
So I was like, okay, let me stay in San Diego and basically like go back and forth, which wasn't that bad.
And it was interesting because I was supporting the CTO who was one of the founders and the chief compute officer.
And so Sam, who was a CTO at the time, he did not want to travel at all.
He was like, I am not going to any hub.
I'm not going to any other city.
Like, I don't want to go meet teams if they come to our staff, if they come to headquarters, that's one thing.
And it was interesting because he had, when I joined, he had just been announced CTO, right?
And so he was having a hard time kind of understanding.
The transition of just being a founder to that leadership role, how important it was for him to kind of like show up to different cities or like go see the teams, like the importance of that.
And so he was like, why don't you go do that for me?
And I was like, I'm not the CTO, but sure.
So it was great because then when we were hiring more like VP, like we don't, Anthropic didn't have like titles per se, like director or anything.
They didn't really believe in that.
They still don't.
So when he hired a lot of his direct report leadership, basically I was like, hey guys, let's go do a tour.
So I was traveling to like London, Dublin, Zurich, Seattle.
We were in Seattle a lot because of Amazon and New York.
So I was constantly traveling regardless.
And then, but yeah, and then I left Anthropic in July and I've just.
I've doing some consulting work.

I actually went to do a project with the Swiss Institute of AI Research in Lugano for like six weeks, about a month ago, basically what they're doing and how they can actually approach the like VC market in the United States, because, you know, it's so different than it is in Europe, right?
And so, but yeah, that's, I've just been just trying, like, you know, just maneuvering through this whole new tech craze.
Yeah, it's nuts.
I know, I know.

built like a small little angel fund together with some backing from the Dutch government.
So just like mainly backing Dutch companies.
Tried to make that a full-time thing, but figured out that that really was not going to be a full-time thing for me at the very least.

My wife is also doing like a whole bunch of things.

called it, didn't want He said he um
So the CEO and CTO here at GrowthX.

So it's just four hours of overlap with San Francisco.
The whole organization is essentially is like fully remote, remote native, and then AI native, which is important.
So essentially it's like everything has like some AI flavor.
And by that, I mean, not, you know, it's like blindly letting, you know, it's like AI dictate everything, but try to try to scale as much expert opinion as possible through, through that.
And the nature of the company is...
Quite different, quite different as well.
So it's essentially Marcel, who has been a CMO at quite a few B2B and specifically is like eng-focused, eng-focused startups.
So he was a CMO at scale, HanashiCorp, a bunch of other companies, and did that quite well.
Predominantly growth and content is his specialty.
And he went on path-leaf, pandemic as well.
Then AI boom started and he started experimenting, doing his job, AI-aided essentially, was able to do everything on a heavily reduced team for his previous employer.
And started taking on, you know, it's like a bunch of additional clients next to that.
And before you know it, he was running a 600K ARR on his own and trying to scale the team in terms of folks that could support him a little bit.

And ultimately started building like a new AI native agency essentially is like codifying his workflows into AI, but mostly it's like building a new operating system for agencies, if you will, that's AI native.
So with a smaller team, do a whole lot more for a wider range of companies and then specifically companies that are also likely on the AI front or the bleeding edge of that.
So the first iterations of the company were essentially just like Airtable plus AirOps plus a bunch of other apps.
That allowed him to do the work that he did more at scale, kind of like tying everything together.
And after a couple of months, that really proved to be a limiter in terms of how he could scale.
So he started looking for a co-founder and found Daniel.
He has a 37signals background.
And he was a fantastic engineer slash designer and started building, or at least like started turning parts of that technology stack into proprietary software.
And that's how we've been building the company, you know, like at least for about a year and a half now, where we have two parts of the company.
One part is the liver, essentially, which is the part of...
A company that interfaces with clients, right?
It's like we take on clients and we do essentially full-service marketing work for them.
And then we have the built part of the company, which is essentially EPD, EPD org plus go-to-market.
So it's the tech org, essentially.
And there's like a couple of functions in there that bridge across, you know, like some parts of engineering will bridge into deliver.
We have forward-deployed engineers.
Some parts of design will bridge into deliver.
We got some brand design, we some marketing.
We have forward-deployed designers.
But essentially we do content marketing and sometimes full-service marketing work for a range of customers.
And each of them is well-paying.
We've scaled up to almost $20 million in ARR now on a relatively small team.
I think we just hit 100 folks now.
That's impressive.
think add to them.
much.

One of the reasons I ask is you have experienced what I had experienced at Lyft and having crucial, certain types of leadership is very crucial, especially with the type of roles that I try to work with, where I'm like, I'm in the weeds more so than most people are.

great relationship and we

so that's why I'm very mindful.

It is, yeah, I think the more challenging thing with growing organizations, especially like in these days, is that because of Gen.AI and especially is like the...
The level at which we expect most people to apply it within our organization is that the lines between roles have blurred a lot.
So understanding when to build upon something or when to take something as directions, these are things that I think we're all struggling with within leadership, but also within communication.
We've just passed that moment between 75 to 100 folks where things don't really, like communication doesn't really traverse anymore because you're working closely to each other.
really have to chase down information now unless it gets broadcasted properly.
So a lot of stuff starts slipping through the cracks now.

The AI, like everything on that side.
